---
title: "Lab 2 — Network Setup"
subtitle: "Understanding and Configuring Linux Networking"
format:
    pdf:
        toc: true
        number-sections: true
        colorlinks: true
        output-file: "Lab 2 - Network Setup.pdf"
---

# Introduction

Before you can attack or defend a networked system, you need to understand how
networks are configured at a low level. In this lab you will learn to read your
current network settings, manually assign an IP address from the terminal, and
restore your original connection — all skills that are essential for the Cyber Storm
competition.

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Concepts at a Glance

| Term | What It Means |
|---|---|
| **IP Address** | A unique numerical label identifying a device on a network |
| **Netmask / Subnet Mask** | Defines which portion of an IP address is the network vs. the host |
| **CIDR Notation** | A compact way to write a network address and its mask (e.g. `10.0.0.0/8`) |
| **DHCP** | A protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses to devices |
| **NAT** | Network Address Translation — a technique that lets many devices share one public IP |
| **Loopback** | A virtual interface (`lo`) that routes packets back to the same machine |
| **Interface** | A network adapter — physical (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) or virtual |
:::

---

# Background: How IP Addressing Works

::: {.callout-note}
## IP Addresses and Subnets

Every device on an IP network needs three pieces of information to communicate:

**1. IP Address**
A 32-bit number (for IPv4) written as four decimal octets separated by dots, e.g.
`138.47.166.224`. This uniquely identifies your machine on the network.

**2. Subnet Mask (Netmask)**
Also a 32-bit number, written the same way, e.g. `255.255.248.0`. The mask divides
the IP address into two parts:

- The **network portion** — bits covered by the `255` octets — identifies the
  network your machine belongs to. All devices on the same network share this prefix.
- The **host portion** — bits covered by the `0` octets — identifies your specific
  machine within that network.

For example, with the address `138.47.166.224` and mask `255.255.248.0`:

- Network: `138.47.160.0` (the first 21 bits are fixed)
- Host: `.166.224` (the last 11 bits are yours)
- Valid host range on this subnet: `138.47.160.1` – `138.47.167.254`

**3. Broadcast Address**
The last address in a subnet (`138.47.167.255` above). A packet sent to the
broadcast address is delivered to every device on the subnet.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## CIDR Notation

Typing `255.255.248.0` is cumbersome. **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)**
notation expresses the same information more concisely by appending a `/` and the
number of network bits to the IP address.

`255.255.248.0` has 21 consecutive `1` bits, so it is written as `/21`.

```
138.47.166.224/21   ←→   IP: 138.47.166.224, Mask: 255.255.248.0
10.0.0.0/8          ←→   IP: 10.0.0.0,       Mask: 255.0.0.0
192.168.1.0/24      ←→   IP: 192.168.1.0,    Mask: 255.255.255.0
```

The `/8`, `/24`, and `/21` tell you how many bits belong to the network — and
therefore how large the subnet is (how many hosts it can hold).
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## DHCP — Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol

When you connect a device to a network, it usually does not have an IP address yet.
**DHCP** solves this automatically:

1. Your device broadcasts a *DHCP Discover* message: "Is there a DHCP server here?"
2. A DHCP server on the network replies with a *DHCP Offer*: "Here, use this IP address."
3. Your device accepts the offer (*DHCP Request*) and the server confirms (*DHCP Ack*).

The server also provides the **subnet mask**, **default gateway**, and **DNS servers**
as part of the same exchange. This entire handshake happens in milliseconds every time
you connect to a network.

In this lab you will bypass DHCP and configure an address manually — this is called
a **static** IP configuration.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## NAT vs. Bridged Adapter (VirtualBox)

When running a virtual machine (VM), the way VirtualBox connects it to the network
matters enormously:

**NAT (Network Address Translation)**

VirtualBox acts as a mini-router between your VM and the outside world. Your VM gets
a private address (usually in the `10.X.Y.Z` range) that only VirtualBox can see.
The host machine translates packets on the VM's behalf. The VM can reach the internet,
but other machines on the real network *cannot reach the VM* by its IP — because that
IP is not visible outside the host.

**Bridged Adapter**

VirtualBox bridges the VM's virtual network card directly onto your physical network.
The VM gets a real IP address from your network's DHCP server (e.g. `138.47.X.Y` on
the Louisiana Tech network) and is visible to other machines just like a physical
device. This is required for Cyber Storm, where your VM must be reachable by other
competitors on the same subnet.

| Mode | VM gets real network IP | Other machines can reach VM | Internet access |
|---|---|---|---|
| NAT | no (private 10.x) | no | yes |
| Bridged | yes | yes | yes |

:::

---

# Using the Terminal

## Checking Your Network Settings

To inspect your current network configuration, run:

```sh
ifconfig
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Command Not Found?

If `ifconfig` is not installed, add it with:

```sh
sudo apt install net-tools
```
:::

The output will look something like this:

```
enp0s8: flags=4163<UP,BROADCAST,RUNNING,MULTICAST>  mtu 1500
        inet 138.47.166.224  netmask 255.255.248.0  broadcast 138.47.167.255
        inet6 fe80::a00:27ff:fe75:a790  prefixlen 64  scopeid 0x20<link>
        ether 08:00:27:75:a7:90  txqueuelen 1000  (Ethernet)
        RX packets 740731  bytes 1041970707 (1.0 GB)
        RX errors 0  dropped 42  overruns 0  frame 0
        TX packets 169179  bytes 16661316 (16.6 MB)
        TX errors 0  dropped 0 overruns 0  carrier 0  collisions 0

lo: flags=73<UP,LOOPBACK,RUNNING>  mtu 65536
        inet 127.0.0.1  netmask 255.0.0.0
        inet6 ::1  prefixlen 128  scopeid 0x10<host>
        loop  txqueuelen 1000  (Local Loopback)
        RX packets 6152  bytes 687551 (687.5 KB)
        RX errors 0  dropped 0  overruns 0  frame 0
        TX packets 6152  bytes 687551 (687.5 KB)
        TX errors 0  dropped 0 overruns 0  carrier 0  collisions 0
```

### Reading the Output

There are two network interfaces listed: `enp0s8` and `lo`.

**`lo` — the Loopback Interface**

The loopback interface is a virtual interface that routes packets straight back to
your own machine without sending anything onto the physical network. It always has
the address `127.0.0.1` (or `::1` for IPv6). You will see this used as `localhost`
in many contexts — for example, when you ran `ftp localhost` or `ssh localhost` in
Lab 3. It is mainly used for local testing and inter-process communication.

**`enp0s8` — the Ethernet Interface**

This is the interface connected to the real network.
**Your interface label may differ** — common alternatives include `eth0`, `enp0s3`,
or `ens33`. The naming convention for modern Linux interface names like `enp0s8` has
three parts:

| Part | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `en` | Ethernet |
| `p0` | PCI bus 0 |
| `s8` | Slot 8 on that bus |

The key fields for this interface are:

```
enp0s8: flags=4163<UP,BROADCAST,RUNNING,MULTICAST>  mtu 1500
        inet 138.47.166.224  netmask 255.255.248.0  broadcast 138.47.167.255
        inet6 fe80::a00:27ff:fe75:a790  prefixlen 64  scopeid 0x20<link>
        ether 08:00:27:75:a7:90  txqueuelen 1000  (Ethernet)
```

| Field | Value (example) | What It Means |
|---|---|---|
| `inet` | `138.47.166.224` | Your IPv4 address |
| `netmask` | `255.255.248.0` | Defines your subnet (`/21` in CIDR) |
| `broadcast` | `138.47.167.255` | Address used to reach all hosts on your subnet |
| `inet6` | `fe80::a00:...` | Your IPv6 link-local address (auto-generated) |
| `ether` | `08:00:27:75:a7:90` | MAC address — a hardware identifier burned into the NIC |
| `RX / TX packets` | — | Counts of packets received / transmitted (useful for diagnosing issues) |

The address `138.47.166.224/21` was assigned automatically by a **DHCP** server on
Louisiana Tech's network.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Troubleshooting: IP Starts with `10.X.Y.Z`?

If your IP address begins with `10`, your VM is in **NAT** mode. Other machines on
the network cannot reach you, which will prevent you from participating in Cyber Storm.

**Fix:**

1. Power off the VM (do **not** save the machine state).
2. In the VirtualBox main window, select the VM and click **Settings**.
3. Go to **Network** → **Adapter 1**.
4. Change *Attached to* from **NAT** to **Bridged Adapter**.
5. Under *Name*, select your **Wi-Fi** adapter (often labeled with `802.11` or `WiFi`).
6. Boot the VM back up and run `ifconfig` again.

On Louisiana Tech's network the address should now begin with `138.47`.
At home it will typically be `192.168.1.X`.
:::

---

## Manually Configuring Your Network Settings

For Cyber Storm, competitors are placed on the private `10.0.0.0/8` network and must
manually assign their own static IP address. DHCP is not available in the competition
environment.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why Manual Configuration?

In competition and penetration testing scenarios, networks are often isolated from
the internet and have no DHCP server. Being able to assign a static IP from memory
— without a GUI — is a fundamental sysadmin and security skill. It also gives you
precise control: you can place yourself on any subnet you choose, which matters when
you need to communicate with specific target machines.
:::

Suppose you want to assign your machine the IP address `10.4.5.6` with a `/8`
netmask. Run the following command, replacing `enp0s8` with your actual interface
name:

```sh
sudo ifconfig enp0s8 10.4.5.6 netmask 255.0.0.0 up
```

Breaking this command down:

| Part | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `sudo` | Run with administrator privileges — required to change network settings |
| `ifconfig enp0s8` | Target this specific network interface |
| `10.4.5.6` | The IP address to assign |
| `netmask 255.0.0.0` | The subnet mask (`/8` — all `10.X.Y.Z` addresses are on the same network) |
| `up` | Bring the interface online after configuring it |

Verify the change by running `ifconfig` again and confirming the new address and
netmask appear for your interface.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Testing That It Worked

If you are doing this during the network setup lab, try visiting a website like
[Google](https://www.google.com) in a browser. If the page **does not load**, that
is expected and correct — you have moved your machine off Louisiana Tech's network
and onto the isolated `10.0.0.0/8` network, which has no internet route.

If the page **does** load, double-check that the IP address changed as expected with
`ifconfig`.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Don't Forget Your Interface Name

Every command in this section that includes `enp0s8` must use **your actual interface
name**. Run `ifconfig` first and note the label of your Ethernet interface before
proceeding.
:::

---

## Switching Back to Louisiana Tech's Network

When you are done with the competition or lab exercise, restore your connection to
Tech's network by resetting the interface and requesting a new address from DHCP.
Run the following three commands in order:

**Step 1 — Remove the current IP address and bring the interface down:**

```sh
sudo ifconfig enp0s8 0.0.0.0 down
```

**Step 2 — Bring the interface back up (with no address yet):**

```sh
sudo ifconfig enp0s8 up
```

**Step 3 — Request a new IP address from the DHCP server:**

```sh
# On Linux Mint:
sudo dhclient enp0s8

# On Ubuntu Server (with systemd-networkd):
sudo systemctl restart systemd-networkd
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Which command do I use in Step 3?

- If you are running **Linux Mint** (or any Ubuntu desktop variant), use `dhclient`.
- If you are running **Ubuntu Server**, use the `systemctl` command.

Not sure? Try `dhclient` first. If it is not installed, fall back to the `systemctl`
command.
:::

After running these commands, verify your address has returned to the `138.47.X.Y`
range (on campus) or `192.168.X.X` (at home) by running `ifconfig`, then confirm
internet access by visiting a website.

---

# Summary

Here is the full sequence of what you can do in this lab, end to end:

| Task | Command |
|---|---|
| View network interfaces | `ifconfig` |
| Assign a static IP | `sudo ifconfig <iface> <ip> netmask <mask> up` |
| Take an interface down | `sudo ifconfig <iface> 0.0.0.0 down` |
| Bring an interface up | `sudo ifconfig <iface> up` |
| Request a DHCP address (Mint) | `sudo dhclient <iface>` |
| Request a DHCP address (Ubuntu Server) | `sudo systemctl restart systemd-networkd` |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Going Further

- **Subnet math:** Practice converting between netmasks and CIDR notation. Given
  `192.168.10.50/26`, can you work out the network address, broadcast address, and
  valid host range?
- **`ip` command:** The `ifconfig` command is considered legacy. The modern
  replacement is the `ip` tool — try `ip addr show` and `ip link set enp0s8 up` to
  see how the syntax differs.
- **Wireshark:** Capture a DHCP handshake by running `sudo dhclient enp0s8` while
  Wireshark is recording. You will see the four-step Discover → Offer → Request → Ack
  exchange in real time.
- **Static persistence:** Changes made with `ifconfig` are temporary and lost on
  reboot. Research how to make a static IP address permanent via Netplan
  (`/etc/netplan/*.yaml`) on Ubuntu or the Network Manager on Mint.
:::
